topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the sterile finality of a closing door. Professor Albright was departing. My carefully constructed world, centered entirely around his approval and guidance, suddenly felt like it was tilting off its axis. I stared at the screen, the fluorescent light of my cramped dorm room buzzing overhead, and a strange, cold knot tightened in my chest. Every perfectly formed sentence I had labored over for months seemed suddenly hollow, a monument to a relationship that was now dissolving. I picked up my pen, the plastic cool against my fingertips, and found myself tracing the outline of the title page, a gesture that felt clumsy and utterly inadequate. I swallowed hard, the dryness in my throat making the simple act of breathing feel like a monumental effort.

[story 2]

"So, that's it?" I managed, the words sounding thin and reedy even to my own ears. The air in the departmental lounge felt thick, suffocating. I watched Professor Albright pack his worn leather satchel, his movements precise and final. A flush crept up my neck, warm and unwelcome. I kept my gaze fixed on my textbook, pretending to absorb the complex theories on the page, but my focus kept fracturing. When he finally glanced my way, there was a flicker of something—perhaps pity, perhaps just acknowledgment—that made my shoulders slump almost imperceptibly. I clutched the strap of my backpack so tightly my knuckles turned white, acutely aware of how small and exposed I felt under the scrutiny of the room.

[story 3]

I walked across the quad, the afternoon sun feeling suddenly harsh and judgmental. The silence around me amplified the frantic drumming in my ears. I kept catching glimpses of Professor Albright across the lawn, laughing with colleagues, his presence radiating an easy confidence that I simply couldn't access anymore. My pace quickened, a desperate attempt to outrun the sudden, sharp pang in my stomach. When I finally reached the library entrance, I stopped, leaning against the cool stone. I closed my eyes, trying to regulate the rapid flutter beneath my ribs. The weight of the impending change pressed down on me, making my vision narrow, as if the entire academic structure I had relied upon was collapsing around my ankles.
